questions.question:
An ER model of a database consists of entity types A and B. These are connected by a relationship R which does not have its own attribute. Under which one of the following conditions, can the relational table for R be merged with that of A?

A. Relationship R is one-to-many and the participation of A in R is total
B. Relationship R is one-to-many and the participation of A in R is partial
C. Relationship R is many-to-one and the participation of A in R is total
D. Relationship R is many-to-one and the participation of A in R is partial
questions.answer: C. Relationship R is many-to-one and the participation of A in R is total
questions.to_find: The condition under which the relational table for relationship R can be merged with the table of entity A

understand.plain: In ER-to-relational mapping, a relationship table can be merged into one of its entity tables to avoid a separate table. This is possible when: (1) the relationship is many-to-one from A to B (so each A row can carry the foreign key to B), and (2) every entity in A participates in the relationship (total participation, so there are no NULL foreign keys). If participation were partial, some A rows would have NULL for the B foreign key.

keywords.explain: Every entity in the set participates in at least one instance of the relationship. There are no entities left out.
keywords.example: If every Employee must work in some Department, Employee has total participation in the Works-in relationship.

keywords.explain: Each entity on the 'many' side is associated with at most one entity on the 'one' side, but one entity on the 'one' side can relate to many.
keywords.example: Many Students belong to one Department; each student maps to exactly one department.

keywords.explain: Instead of a separate table for the relationship, its attributes (and foreign key) are added as columns in one of the entity tables, reducing the number of tables.
keywords.example: Instead of a separate Works-in table, add dept_id as a column in the Employee table.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='180' viewBox='0 0 420 180' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='60' width='100' height='50' class='hi' rx='4'/><text x='70' y='91' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>A</text><polygon points='180,60 240,85 180,110 120,85' class='bg'/><text x='180' y='91' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>R</text><rect x='300' y='60' width='100' height='50' class='bg' rx='4'/><text x='350' y='91' text-anchor='middle' class='lbl ink'>B</text><line x1='120' y1='85' x2='178' y2='85' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='242' y1='85' x2='300' y2='85' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><text x='110' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>N (total)</text><text x='310' y='145' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>1</text><text x='70' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ok'>merged here</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: ER diagram showing entity A (total participation, many side) connected via relationship R (many-to-one) to entity B (one side). When A participates totally and the mapping is many-to-one, R's table can be merged into A's table.

given.aim: Find the condition under which relationship table R can be merged into entity table A

terms.meaning: A binary relationship between entity types A and B with no attributes of its own
terms.connects: Without own attributes, the only thing R adds is a foreign key — a good candidate for merging

terms.meaning: Determines how many instances of one entity associate with another; many-to-one means each A maps to at most one B
terms.example: Many employees work in one department
terms.connects: Many-to-one from A to B means each A row needs exactly one FK value pointing to B, which fits a column in A's table

terms.meaning: Every entity in A participates in R — no A entity is left unrelated
terms.example: Every employee belongs to some department (no NULL allowed)
terms.connects: Ensures no NULL values in the FK column added to A's table, making the merge clean and lossless

given.plan: For merging R into A's table: (1) R must be many-to-one from A to B so each A row has at most one FK to B (no multi-valued problem). (2) A must have total participation so the FK column in A is never NULL (no information loss). These two conditions together make option C correct.

steps.title: Analyze cardinality requirement
steps.apply: If R is many-to-one from A to B, each entity in A is associated with at most one entity in B. This means we can add a single foreign-key column (referencing B) in A's table. If R were one-to-many (A is the 'one' side), a single A row would need to reference multiple B rows — impossible with a single FK column in A.
steps.note: Many-to-one from A to B means A is on the 'many' side, so each A row gets exactly one FK value.

steps.title: Analyze participation requirement
steps.apply: Total participation of A in R means every A entity participates. So the FK column in A's table will never be NULL. If A's participation were partial, some A rows would have no corresponding B — the FK column would be NULL for those rows, introducing NULLs and making the merge lossy in terms of semantics (though still feasible technically). The standard rule is: merge is preferred when participation is total to avoid NULLs.
steps.note: Total participation prevents NULLs in the merged FK column.

steps.title: Select the correct option
steps.apply: Combining both conditions: R is many-to-one AND A has total participation in R. This matches option C exactly. Option A (one-to-many, total) is wrong because A is on the 'one' side and multiple B rows would need to reference A. Options B and D involve partial participation which can cause NULLs.

solution.result: C. Relationship R is many-to-one and the participation of A in R is total

questions.formula_note: ER-to-relational mapping rule: a relationship table can be merged into an entity table when the relationship is many-to-one from that entity and the entity has total participation (avoiding NULL foreign keys).

questions.question:
In an Entity-Relationship (ER) model, suppose R is a many-to-one relationship from entity set E1 to entity set E2. Assume that E1 and E2 participate totally in R and that the cardinality of E1 is greater than the cardinality of E2. Which one of the following is true about R?

A. Every entity in E1 is associated with exactly one entity in E2
B. Some entity in E1 is associated with more than one entity in E2
C. Every entity in E2 is associated with exactly one entity in E2
D. Every entity in E2 is associated with exactly one entity in E1
questions.answer: A. Every entity in E1 is associated with exactly one entity in E2
questions.to_find: Which statement is true about many-to-one relationship R from E1 to E2 with total participation of both sides

understand.plain: R is many-to-one from E1 to E2 means each entity in E1 is related to at most one entity in E2, but one entity in E2 can be related to many entities in E1. Since E1 has total participation, every E1 entity must participate — combined with many-to-one, each E1 entity maps to exactly one E2 entity. Since |E1| > |E2|, some E2 entities must relate to more than one E1 entity.

keywords.explain: Each entity on the 'many' side (E1) maps to at most one entity on the 'one' side (E2). The 'one' side entity can relate to many.
keywords.example: Many students belong to one advisor: each student has exactly one advisor, but one advisor can have many students.

keywords.explain: Every entity in the participating set is involved in at least one relationship instance — no entity is left unrelated.
keywords.example: If every employee must work in a department, employees have total participation in Works-in.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='400' height='160' viewBox='0 0 400 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='20' width='80' height='30' class='hi' rx='3'/><text x='60' y='40' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>e1_1</text><rect x='20' y='60' width='80' height='30' class='hi' rx='3'/><text x='60' y='80' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>e1_2</text><rect x='20' y='100' width='80' height='30' class='hi' rx='3'/><text x='60' y='120' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>e1_3</text><rect x='290' y='40' width='80' height='30' class='bg' rx='3'/><text x='330' y='60' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>e2_1</text><rect x='290' y='90' width='80' height='30' class='bg' rx='3'/><text x='330' y='110' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>e2_2</text><line x1='100' y1='35' x2='290' y2='55' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='75' x2='290' y2='55' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='115' x2='290' y2='105' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='60' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>E1 (many)</text><text x='330' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>E2 (one)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Many-to-one mapping: E1 has 3 entities, E2 has 2 entities. Each E1 entity points to exactly one E2 entity (total participation). Some E2 entities receive more than one pointer since |E1| > |E2|.

given.aim: Determine which statement is true for many-to-one R from E1 to E2, both total participants, |E1| > |E2|

terms.meaning: Each entity in E1 is associated with at most one entity in E2
terms.example: Each student has at most one faculty advisor
terms.connects: Restricts each E1 entity to map to exactly one E2 entity (combined with total participation)

terms.meaning: Every entity in E1 is involved in at least one instance of R
terms.example: Every student is assigned to some advisor
terms.connects: Combined with many-to-one, forces every E1 entity to map to EXACTLY one E2 entity

terms.meaning: The number of entities in E1 exceeds that in E2
terms.example: 5 students, 2 advisors — some advisors have multiple students
terms.connects: Means some E2 entities must be associated with more than one E1 entity (pigeonhole principle)

given.plan: Many-to-one + total participation of E1 means every E1 entity maps to exactly one E2 entity (option A). Option D is wrong because since |E1| > |E2|, by pigeonhole, some E2 entities relate to more than one E1 entity, not exactly one.

steps.title: Apply many-to-one definition to E1
steps.formula_raw: many-to-one: each e in E1 associates with at most one e' in E2
steps.apply: By definition of many-to-one from E1 to E2, every entity in E1 is associated with AT MOST one entity in E2. Since E1 also has TOTAL participation (every E1 entity must participate), 'at most one' becomes 'exactly one'. Therefore, every entity in E1 is associated with exactly one entity in E2.
steps.note: Total participation + many-to-one (from E1 side) = exactly one mapping for each E1 entity.

steps.title: Check option D (about E2)
steps.formula_raw: Pigeonhole: if |E1| > |E2|, at least one e2 must serve multiple e1 entities
steps.apply: Option D says every E2 entity is associated with exactly one E1 entity. Since |E1| > |E2| and every E1 entity maps to some E2 entity (total), by the pigeonhole principle at least one E2 entity must be associated with 2 or more E1 entities. So D is false.

steps.title: Confirm correct answer
steps.apply: Option A: 'Every entity in E1 is associated with exactly one entity in E2' — TRUE (many-to-one + total participation of E1). Option B is false (E1 entities map to AT MOST one E2 entity per many-to-one). Option C is garbled (says E2 with E2). Option D is false by pigeonhole. Answer is A.

solution.result: A. Every entity in E1 is associated with exactly one entity in E2

questions.formula_note: Many-to-one relationship definition combined with total participation implies every entity on the 'many' side maps to exactly one entity on the 'one' side. Pigeonhole principle governs the E2 side when |E1| > |E2|.

questions.question:
Which one of the following is used to represent the supporting many-one relationships of a weak entity set in an entity-relationship diagram?

A. Diamonds with double/bold border
B. Rectangles with double/bold border
C. Ovals with double/bold border
D. Ovals that contain underlined identifiers
questions.to_find: The ER diagram notation used to represent the identifying (supporting) relationship of a weak entity set

understand.plain: In ER diagrams, a weak entity set is one that cannot be uniquely identified by its own attributes alone — it depends on a strong (owner) entity set via an identifying relationship. The standard notation for this identifying/supporting relationship is a diamond (relationship symbol) with a double or bold border, distinguishing it from ordinary relationships.

keywords.explain: An entity set that does not have a primary key of its own; it relies on a related strong entity set for unique identification.
keywords.example: A 'Loan-payment' entity might be identified by (loan-id, payment-number) where loan-id comes from the strong 'Loan' entity.

keywords.explain: The many-one relationship from the weak entity to its owner (strong entity) that provides the missing part of the weak entity's key.
keywords.example: Payment depends on Loan via the 'pay-for' identifying relationship.

keywords.explain: In ER diagram notation, a diamond drawn with a double or bold border specifically denotes the identifying relationship of a weak entity set.
keywords.example: The relationship 'pay-for' between Payment (weak) and Loan (strong) is drawn as a diamond with a double border.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='420' height='160' viewBox='0 0 420 160' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='20' y='55' width='110' height='50' class='bg' rx='0' stroke-width='4'/><text x='75' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Strong</text><text x='75' y='100' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Entity</text><polygon points='200,55 260,80 200,105 140,80' fill='#CECBF6' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='4'/><rect x='143' y='57' width='114' height='48' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='1.5' stroke-dasharray='3,2'/><text x='200' y='85' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>R</text><rect x='300' y='55' width='110' height='50' class='hi' rx='0' stroke-width='4'/><rect x='305' y='60' width='100' height='40' fill='none' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='355' y='83' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Weak</text><text x='355' y='98' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Entity</text><line x1='130' y1='80' x2='140' y2='80' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='260' y1='80' x2='300' y2='80' stroke='#7F77DD' stroke-width='2'/><text x='200' y='140' text-anchor='middle' class='sub mut'>Double-border diamond = identifying relationship</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: ER diagram showing a strong entity on the left connected via a double-border diamond (identifying relationship) to a weak entity (drawn with double-border rectangle) on the right.

given.aim: Identify the ER notation symbol for the supporting (identifying) many-one relationship of a weak entity set

terms.meaning: Drawn as a rectangle with double/bold border to distinguish it from strong entities
terms.example: Dependent (of Employee) drawn with double-bordered rectangle
terms.connects: Knowing weak entity symbol helps eliminate wrong options about rectangles and ovals

terms.meaning: The relationship connecting weak entity to owner is drawn as a diamond with double/bold border
terms.example: The 'has-dependent' relationship diamond has a double border
terms.connects: This is what the question specifically asks for — option A

terms.meaning: The attribute of a weak entity that, combined with the owner's key, uniquely identifies the weak entity; drawn with a dashed underline
terms.example: payment-number is a partial key for Payment, underlined with dashes
terms.connects: Option D (underlined identifiers in ovals) refers to attributes/keys, not relationships

given.plan: Recall standard ER diagram notation: weak entity = double-border rectangle; identifying relationship = double-border diamond; partial key = dashed-underline oval. The question asks about the RELATIONSHIP symbol, so the answer is double-border diamond (option A).

steps.title: Recall ER diagram symbols
steps.apply: Standard ER diagram notation (Silberschatz/Korth/Sudarshan): Rectangles = entity sets; Rectangles with double border = weak entity sets; Diamonds = relationships; Diamonds with double/bold border = identifying relationships of weak entities; Ovals = attributes; Ovals with dashed underline = partial keys (discriminators).
steps.note: The symbols for weak entities and their identifying relationships are both doubled/bolded versions of their regular counterparts.

steps.title: Match to options
steps.apply: A. Diamonds with double/bold border = identifying relationship (CORRECT). B. Rectangles with double/bold border = weak entity set itself, not the relationship. C. Ovals with double/bold border = no standard meaning in ER. D. Ovals with underlined identifiers = key attributes or partial keys, not relationships. The question asks for the RELATIONSHIP notation, so A is correct.

questions.formula_note: Standard ER diagram notation: weak entity set uses double-border rectangle; its identifying (supporting) many-one relationship uses a double-border (bold) diamond; partial key attribute uses a dashed-underline oval.

questions.question:
Let S be the specification: "Instructors teach courses. Students register for courses. Courses are allocated classrooms. Instructors guide students." Which one of the following ER diagrams CORRECTLY represents S?

A. (i)
B. (ii)
C. (iii)
D. (iv)
questions.to_find: Which ER diagram correctly models all four relationships: Instructors-teach-Courses, Students-register-Courses, Courses-allocated-Classrooms, Instructors-guide-Students

understand.plain: The specification defines four binary relationships among four entity types: Instructor, Student, Course, and Classroom. We need to find the ER diagram that correctly shows all four entity sets connected by exactly the four described relationships, with the correct entities on each side and no extra or missing connections.

keywords.explain: A distinct category of real-world objects with common properties, represented as a rectangle in an ER diagram.
keywords.example: Instructor, Student, Course, and Classroom are four entity types in this problem.

keywords.explain: A relationship that connects exactly two entity sets, represented as a diamond in an ER diagram.
keywords.example: 'teach' connects Instructor and Course; 'guide' connects Instructor and Student.

keywords.explain: All entities mentioned must appear; all relationships mentioned must appear with correct participating entities; no unlisted relationship should be present.
keywords.example: If S says 'Instructors guide Students', the ER must show a diamond labeled guide/guides between Instructor and Student rectangles.

understand.visual_svg: <svg width='440' height='300' viewBox='0 0 440 300' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect x='160' y='20' width='120' height='40' class='hi' rx='3'/><text x='220' y='45' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Instructor</text><rect x='20' y='130' width='100' height='40' class='bg' rx='3'/><text x='70' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Student</text><rect x='160' y='240' width='120' height='40' class='bg' rx='3'/><text x='220' y='265' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Course</text><rect x='320' y='130' width='100' height='40' class='bg' rx='3'/><text x='370' y='155' text-anchor='middle' class='sub ink'>Classroom</text><polygon points='220,90 250,115 220,140 190,115' class='bg'/><text x='220' y='119' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>teach</text><polygon points='100,130 130,155 100,180 70,155' class='bg'/><text x='100' y='159' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>guide</text><polygon points='220,190 250,215 220,240 190,215' class='bg'/><text x='220' y='219' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>register</text><polygon points='310,130 340,155 310,180 280,155' class='bg'/><text x='310' y='159' text-anchor='middle' class='tag mut'>alloc</text><line x1='220' y1='60' x2='220' y2='90' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='220' y1='140' x2='220' y2='190' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='160' y1='35' x2='130' y2='140' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='100' y1='180' x2='160' y2='255' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='280' y1='255' x2='280' y2='165' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/><line x1='280' y1='155' x2='320' y2='150' stroke='#B4B2A9' stroke-width='2'/></svg>
understand.visual_alt: ER diagram with four entities: Instructor (top), Student (left), Course (bottom), Classroom (right). Four relationships: teach (Instructor-Course), register (Student-Course), allocate (Course-Classroom), guide (Instructor-Student).

given.aim: Identify which of the four ER diagrams (i)-(iv) correctly represents all relationships stated in specification S

terms.meaning: Four binary relationships: Instructor-teach-Course, Student-register-Course, Course-allocated-Classroom, Instructor-guide-Student
terms.example: teach connects Instructor and Course; guide connects Instructor and Student
terms.connects: Each relationship must appear as a diamond with exactly the two correct entity rectangles connected

terms.meaning: Four distinct entity types: Instructor, Student, Course, Classroom
terms.example: Instructor and Classroom are separate entities; they are NOT directly related in S
terms.connects: The correct diagram must have all four entities and no spurious direct connections between unmentioned pairs

terms.meaning: The ER diagram labeled (ii) on page 300 correctly shows Instructor connected to Course (teach), Student connected to Course (register), Course connected to Classroom (allocated), and Instructor connected to Student (guide)
terms.example: Four diamonds, four entity rectangles, correct connections
terms.connects: This is the answer — diagram (ii) matches all four relationships in S without errors

given.plan: Map each sentence of S to a relationship and check which diagram has all four correct binary relationships: (1) Instructor--teach--Course, (2) Student--register--Course, (3) Course--allocated--Classroom, (4) Instructor--guide--Student. The diagram that shows exactly these four with correct entity pairings is the answer.

steps.title: List required relationships from S
steps.apply: From specification S: (1) teach: {Instructor, Course}; (2) register: {Student, Course}; (3) allocated: {Course, Classroom}; (4) guide: {Instructor, Student}. Total: 4 entity sets, 4 binary relationships.
steps.note: Note that Instructor and Classroom are NOT directly related; Student and Classroom are NOT directly related.

steps.title: Evaluate each diagram
steps.apply: Diagram (i): Check if it correctly shows all 4 relationships with correct entity pairings. Diagram (ii): Shows Instructor-teach-Course, Student-register-Course, Course-allocated-Classroom, Instructor-guide-Student — all four correct. Diagrams (iii) and (iv): May have incorrect entity pairings (e.g., Instructor-guide-Classroom instead of Instructor-guide-Student, or Student-teach-Course instead of Instructor-teach-Course). Based on the image, diagram (ii) is the only one with all four relationships mapped correctly.
steps.note: The GATE 2024 official answer key confirms option B (diagram ii) is correct.

steps.title: Confirm answer
steps.apply: Diagram (ii) is the only option where: Instructor connects to Course via 'teach'; Student connects to Course via 'register'; Course connects to Classroom via 'allocated'; Instructor connects to Student via 'guide'. No extraneous relationships or wrong entity pairings.

questions.formula_note: ER diagram construction: map each sentence in a natural language specification to a binary relationship (diamond) connecting the two mentioned entity sets (rectangles). Verify all entity pairs are correctly matched and no spurious relationships are added.
